title: Scale Robot Policy Evaluation with Ray | Anyscale
gist: Anyscale and Ray solve the challenge of scaling robot foundation model evaluation by disaggregating GPU-heavy policy inference (served via Ray Serve) from GPU-heavy physics simulation (Isaac Lab), allowing each to scale independently. Using NVIDIA's GR00T-N1.7-3B VLA and a Unitree G1 humanoid, a single policy fleet can serve hundreds of concurrent simulator rollouts, turning evaluation from a serial hardware bottleneck into an autoscaling inference service. The architectural pattern mirrors how LLM inference is scaled in production.

themes.theme: Robotics Sim-Eval as a Distributed Systems Problem

insights.claim: Evaluating robot foundation models at scale is primarily a distributed-systems infrastructure problem, not a modeling problem, because running GPU-heavy simulation and GPU-heavy policy inference together across hundreds of rollouts spans multiple GPUs and nodes.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Companies building robotics AI face an infrastructure scaling bottleneck that mirrors LLM serving, creating demand for distributed compute platforms like Anyscale.
insights.quote: Evaluating robot foundation models means running GPU-heavy simulation and GPU-heavy policy inference together across hundreds or thousands of rollouts. At scale this spans multiple GPU and nodes.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Real-robot evaluation is slow, expensive, and unsafe, making simulation-based evaluation (sim-eval) a critical tool for robotics model development despite introducing distributed-systems complexity.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Simulation infrastructure vendors and GPU cloud providers stand to benefit as robotics teams shift evaluation workloads from physical robots to cloud-scale simulation.
insights.quote: Simulation, in contrast, is safe, repeatable, parallelizable, and cheap per trial. So evaluation in simulation (sim-eval) is an important tool.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Reproducibility of the robotics software stack—spanning Isaac Lab, GR00T, a gated VLM backbone, pinned versions of transformers and flash-attn, and runtime patches—is what actually gates evaluation throughput for most robotics teams, not the modeling itself.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Managed ML platform vendors that solve reproducibility for complex heterogeneous stacks (e.g., Anyscale) have a strong value proposition in the robotics market.
insights.quote: In most robotics teams this reproducibility problem, not the modeling, is what actually gates evaluation throughput.
insights.locator: Part 3: Policy as a Service

insights.claim: Co-locating a 3B+ VLA model and Isaac Sim physics/rendering on the same GPU causes VRAM and compute contention that stalls both workloads, requiring disaggregation into separate GPU processes communicating over HTTP.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Robotics AI inference requires at minimum two GPUs per rollout if co-located, dramatically increasing per-evaluation hardware cost and favoring cloud-scale deployments.
insights.quote: A 3B vision-language-action (VLA) model wants a GPU, and Isaac Sim's physics and rendering want a GPU. Co-locate them and they contend for memory and compute, stalling each other.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Running each simulator as an isolated Ray task with its own GPU and Python process, communicating with the policy over HTTP, eliminates GPU/runtime contention without requiring each rollout to load its own copy of the 3B model.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: This disaggregation pattern significantly improves GPU utilization efficiency for robotics evaluation workloads, reducing total GPU hours needed per evaluation run.
insights.quote: Run each simulator as an isolated process separate from policy inference, a Ray task that gets its own clean Python interpreter and event loop, and have it talk to the policy over the HTTP endpoint.
insights.locator: Part 2: The simulator and the policy cannot share a process or a GPU

insights.claim: Policy and simulator scale independently in the disaggregated architecture—adding policy replicas fixes inference bottlenecks while adding simulator workers fixes rollout throughput bottlenecks, with no changes to the other side's code.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Independent scaling of inference and simulation is a key cost efficiency lever, enabling teams to right-size GPU allocation to the actual bottleneck.
insights.quote: Simulators and policy replicas scale independently. If rollouts are starved waiting on inference, Ray Serve can autoscale the policy fleet by adding replicas.
insights.locator: Part 3: Policy as a Service

insights.claim: GR00T-N1.7-3B's single forward pass takes approximately 1.6 seconds and returns a 40-step action chunk, allowing one policy inference call to cover multiple physics simulation steps and dramatically increasing the ratio of simulators a single policy replica can serve.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Action chunking is a key economic lever: with an action_horizon of 8, one policy replica can serve 8x more simulators than a naive query-every-step design, directly reducing GPU costs.
insights.quote: Round trip: 1641 ms
insights.locator: Part 1: Closed-loop evaluation needs online policy serving

insights.claim: With action_horizon set to 8 (executing 8 of GR00T's 40 available action steps per inference call), a single ~1.6-second inference call covers eight physics steps, allowing a small fleet of policy replicas to keep many concurrent rollouts fed.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Action chunk sizing is a tunable efficiency parameter that determines the inference-to-simulation GPU cost ratio, a critical operational metric for at-scale robotics evaluation.
insights.quote: With action_horizon = 8, one ~1.6-second inference covers eight physics steps, so the GPU running the policy serves eight times as many simulators as a naive query-every-step loop would.
insights.locator: Part 2: The simulator and the policy cannot share a process or a GPU

themes.theme: Ray Serve as Production Policy Infrastructure

insights.claim: Ray Serve treats a robot foundation model policy identically to an LLM serving deployment—wrapping it as an HTTP microservice with health checks, versioning, and horizontal autoscaling—making the same infrastructure patterns applicable to both domains.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: LLM serving infrastructure investments (Ray Serve, vLLM, etc.) are directly transferable to robotics evaluation, lowering adoption barriers for teams already on these platforms.
insights.quote: This is the same pattern used to serve large language models in production. The policy is now an ordinary HTTP microservice. It's versioned, health-checked, and horizontally scalable.
insights.locator: Part 1: Closed-loop evaluation needs online policy serving

insights.claim: Scaling a Ray Serve policy fleet from 1 to 4 replicas requires a single line of code change (num_replicas=4), with Ray Serve automatically scheduling each replica on its own GPU and load-balancing client requests.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Low operational overhead for scaling policy replicas makes Ray Serve attractive for robotics teams needing rapid iteration across evaluation sweeps.
insights.quote: Scaling that fleet is one line. Ray Serve schedules each replica on its own GPU, and clients load-balance across them automatically.
insights.locator: Part 1: Closed-loop evaluation needs online policy serving

insights.claim: A 3B model load exceeds Ray Serve's default 30-second health-check timeout, requiring explicit extension (health_check_timeout_s=300) when deploying large VLA models in production.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Non-obvious configuration pitfalls with large models highlight the value of managed platforms (Anyscale) that pre-configure these parameters.
insights.quote: a 3B model takes longer to load than Ray Serve's default 30-second health-check window, so we extend it
insights.locator: Part 1: Closed-loop evaluation needs online policy serving

themes.theme: NVIDIA GR00T Ecosystem and Humanoid Robot AI

insights.claim: NVIDIA's GR00T-N1.7-3B VLA model outputs 9-dimensional end-effector pose vectors (3 translation + 6 rotation matrix values) and 40-step action chunks covering every actuated part of the Unitree G1 humanoid body including arms, hands, waist, base height, and navigation.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: GR00T's multi-modal, whole-body action representation indicates NVIDIA is targeting full-body humanoid control, not just arm manipulation, expanding the addressable market for GR00T-based deployments.
insights.quote: The policy returns a 40-step action chunk covering every actuated part of the body.
insights.locator: Part 1: Closed-loop evaluation needs online policy serving

insights.claim: NVIDIA offers a task-specific fine-tune (nvidia/GR00T-N1.6-G1-PnPAppleToPlate) post-trained on a pick-and-place task, accessible as a one-line model path swap within the same Ray Serve deployment framework.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: NVIDIA's expanding catalog of task-specific GR00T fine-tunes signals a hub-and-spoke model strategy that drives repeated evaluation workloads and ongoing GPU compute demand.
insights.quote: moving from a base model to a task fine-tune (for example NVIDIA's nvidia/GR00T-N1.6-G1-PnPAppleToPlate, post-trained on this exact pick-and-place task is a one-line change
insights.locator: What's Next

insights.claim: The GR00T-to-Isaac Lab integration requires non-trivial schema translation: observations must convert to GR00T's REAL_G1 modality dict with 2-frame ego_view stacks and 9-D end-effector poses, while GR00T's 9-key 40-step action chunk must be flattened to Isaac Lab's flat (1, 28) joint command format.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Schema translation complexity creates integration overhead that favors managed platforms or middleware solutions bridging VLA models and simulators.
insights.quote: bridging them is the genuinely fiddly part of wiring any VLA into a simulator
insights.locator: Part 2: The simulator and the policy cannot share a process or a GPU

insights.claim: Robotics evaluation is converging with LLM inference infrastructure: both require distributed GPU inference fleets, heterogeneous scheduling, autoscaling services, and tightly coordinated closed-loop workloads, making the same Ray primitives applicable to both domains.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: LLM infrastructure platform vendors (Anyscale, Modal, Together AI) have a direct expansion path into robotics evaluation without major architecture changes.
insights.quote: The same distributed systems patterns that scaled LLM inference are now becoming necessary for robotics simulation and evaluation.
insights.locator: The payoff: evaluation as a scalable service

insights.claim: The same Ray Serve primitives that scale LLM inference to thousands of concurrent requests can scale robot-policy evaluation to thousands of concurrent rollouts, removing the need to rebuild infrastructure when moving from language to robotics AI.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Platform stickiness for AI infrastructure vendors increases as the same tooling covers both LLM and robotics workloads, reducing customer incentive to switch.
insights.quote: The same Ray primitives that scale LLM inference to thousands of concurrent requests scale robot-policy evaluation to thousands of concurrent rollouts.
insights.locator: The payoff: evaluation as a scalable service

insights.claim: As robotics foundation models grow larger and simulation workloads grow in realism, the key architectural shift is disaggregation—policies and simulators scaling independently through Ray Serve and Ray Core—mirroring how modern AI serving evolved.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Disaggregated inference architecture is becoming the dominant pattern for both LLM and robotics AI, favoring infrastructure platforms built around this model (Anyscale, Ray).
insights.quote: Robotics evaluation is evolving into an infrastructure problem. As robotics foundation models grow larger and simulation workloads grow in realism, evaluation begins to resemble modern AI serving.
insights.locator: The payoff: evaluation as a scalable service

insights.claim: Anyscale packages the entire heterogeneous robotics stack (Isaac Lab, GR00T, gated VLM backbone, pinned library versions, runtime patches) into one reproducible cluster image, enabling teams to move from a single rollout to hundreds of parallel rollouts without rebuilding infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Managed reproducibility of complex multi-library robotics stacks is a differentiated enterprise value proposition that justifies platform premium pricing.
insights.quote: Anyscale packages this heterogeneous, notoriously brittle robotics stack into one reproducible cluster image and runs it on a managed Ray cluster.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Runtime environment credentials (Hugging Face tokens for gated model weights) propagate automatically across the Anyscale cluster to both policy replicas and simulator tasks without manual per-node setup.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Automated credential propagation reduces DevOps friction for gated model deployments, a real operational pain point that adds to Anyscale's enterprise value proposition.
insights.quote: The same runtime_env mechanism contains the gated token for the Serve replica and is handed to every simulator task, so credentials propagate across the cluster without manual setup.
insights.locator: Part 3: Policy as a Service

insights.claim: Anyscale can autoscale the underlying GPU cluster to match demand as simulator workers are fanned out, decoupling evaluation scale from pre-provisioned hardware capacity.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Dynamic GPU cluster autoscaling is critical for cost-efficient burst evaluation workloads, making cloud-based platforms preferable to fixed on-premise GPU clusters for robotics teams.
insights.quote: with Anyscale scaling the underlying GPU cluster to match demand
insights.locator: Part 3: Policy as a Service

insights.claim: Robot policy evaluation is inherently a closed-loop problem—policy inference and simulation alternate step-by-step with outputs feeding inputs—making it impossible to decouple into independent offline batch pipeline stages.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Closed-loop requirements eliminate offline batch processing architectures and require low-latency online serving infrastructure, increasing GPU infrastructure complexity and cost.
insights.quote: It is a closed loop, not a batch pass. Policy inference and simulation alternate, step by step, with the output of one feeding the input of the other. You cannot decouple them into independent offline stages.
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Hundreds of rollouts should share one policy fleet rather than each loading its own copy of the 3B+ model, as naive scaling would require N copies of a 3B+ model per rollout—wasteful and often infeasible on GPU memory.
insights.speaker: Anyscale / Ray
insights.so_what: Shared policy serving is not just an optimization but a requirement for multi-hundred rollout evaluation due to GPU memory constraints, making inference microservice patterns mandatory at scale.
insights.quote: Combine the two and scaling rollouts means standing up N copies of a 3B+ model, which is wasteful and often infeasible on GPU memory.
insights.locator: Introduction
